Embark on a captivating journey to the vibrant city of Belfast, Northern Ireland, as we unveil the top things to do and see in this captivating destination. This immersive video will take you on a virtual tour of Belfast's rich history, stunning architecture, and lively atmosphere.

Explore the fascinating history of Belfast at the Titanic Belfast, an award-winning museum that tells the story of the ill-fated RMS Titanic. Learn about the city's maritime heritage, explore interactive exhibits, and even step aboard a full-scale replica of the Titanic's grand staircase.

Discover the iconic political murals that adorn the city's streets, reflecting Belfast's complex past. Take a guided tour through the neighborhoods to gain insights into the Troubles and the city's journey towards peace and reconciliation.

Indulge in Belfast's vibrant food scene at local pubs, restaurants, and markets. Sample traditional dishes such as Ulster Fry, Irish stew, and soda bread, or explore the city's diverse culinary offerings from around the world.

Explore the stunning landscapes surrounding Belfast, such as the Giant's Causeway. Marvel at the otherworldly basalt columns and take in the breathtaking views of the North Antrim Coast.

Experience the lively atmosphere of Belfast's cultural scene at the Cathedral Quarter. Discover art galleries, theaters, and live music venues that showcase the city's creative talents.

Immerse yourself in the history of the impressive Belfast City Hall, an architectural masterpiece set in the heart of the city. Take a guided tour to explore its ornate interiors and learn about its significance in Belfast's history.

Visit the historic Crumlin Road Gaol, now a fascinating museum. Delve into the prison's dark past, hear stories of its inmates, and even take a guided tour through the underground tunnel that connected the gaol to the courthouse.

Join us as we uncover the hidden gems and must-visit attractions of Belfast, Northern Ireland's vibrant capital. Let us transport you to a world of captivating beauty, historical significance, and unforgettable experiences.

Get ready to be captivated by the allure of Belfast in this immersive video experience. So, pack your bags, and let the adventure begin in this remarkable city where history, culture, and warm hospitality await!

videos.title: “Trying To BEHEAD!” | UK Reacts After Graphic Belfast Knife Attack Leaves Man Critical
videos.description:
A Sudanese migrant has been arrested on suspicion of attempted murder after a brutal knife attack in north Belfast. The victim, a man in his 40s, suffered serious injuries to his face, neck and back and remains in hospital.

Police say there is no evidence of terrorism. The suspect reportedly received leave to remain in Northern Ireland after crossing from Dublin. Political leaders condemned the attack and urged the public not to share graphic footage.

Kevin O’Sullivan interviews Chief Political Writer for Spiked Online, Brendan O’Neill.

#belfast #northernireland  #KnifeAttack #CrimeNews #UKNews #Immigration #Migration #ChannelMigrants #BorderForce #Police #PSNI #BreakingNews

Have you got a story? Email tips@talk.tv

Uh, now you asked this question on Instagram this morning. I couldn't help noticing it. I'm going to answer it for you. You said, "Are we allowed to feel pure cold rage yet?" In other words, quoting what Nigel Farage said about the Henry Novak saga. Uh, I'm going to answer your question is no, we are not allowed to feel cold rage. Uh, pure cold rage. Uh, Kia Starma will tell us we're not allowed to politicize a man, a Sudin man trying to be behead a local Northern Irish guy in Belfast. We're not allowed to do this. And to do so will make us Neanderal and far right. It's going to happen again, isn't it? >> Absolutely right. I I pose that very much as a rhetorical question because as you suggest, we know the answer. We know what the political class is already thinking. They're already thinking, "Oh god, how are ordinary people going to respond to this? Are they going to turn into a racist mob? Are they all going to go absolutely crazy like they tend to do?" That whenever something like this happens, whenever there is this kind of barbaric violence carried out by people who should not be in this country, that's their first instinct. Their first instinct is not to worry about the victim or not to worry about our broken borders or not to worry about the fact that they have overseen the arrival of thousands of unvetted men. Instead, they worry about us and our emotions and our words and what we are going to do. And that's exactly what's going to happen here. >> Exactly right. Uh they will do this again. They will do this. Uh and knowing Star, he'll probably be have his fingers crossed hoping there'll be a lot of rioting in Belfast tonight. We all hope, by the way, there isn't, but there's a massive protest planned. If there's a riot, you know what Starmmer's going to do? Disgraceful scenes. You know, I you know, I'm gonna arrest them, send them all to jail. They'll shift the narrative away from the extraordinary shocking fact that a man from Sudan tried to ritually behead a Northern Irish guy on a residential street in Belfast last night. >> It's beyond horrific. And you are absolutely right that they will do everything they can to deflect attention from the story here which is the barbarism that we saw on the streets of Belfast. Let's be honest about this. This was an apocalyptic act of savagery. This was an unspeakable crime. It was beyond belief almost like a form of medieval butchery on the streets of Belfast in 2026. If you read what the police said this morning, they said the man has significant injuries to his eyes. This was an attempted eye gouging in public in Britain in 2026. It is beyond repulsive and that's the only thing we should be talking about. But you know what Kevin, we've got to move. We've got to look at who else is responsible for this. Now, of course, the only one who bears responsibility for the awful uh injuries inflicted on that man is the disgusting knife man. But he was aided and abetted by the state. He was aided and abetted by the willfully blind technocratic class who have overseen the destruction of our borders, by the activist class that is constantly crowing refugees welcome because they care more about their own virtue than they do about the safety of working-class men and women. And by a legal system that just completely refuses to remove people who should not be in this country. So be the backdrop to this crime is the state failure. the failure of the state to protect our borders, to remove people who shouldn't be here, and to protect ordinary Britons. That's what this story is about. >> Exactly. Right. And of course, uh, this government, David Lammy and Starman, they spent a lot of time condemning JD Vance for his take on the Henry Novak horror, uh, because he linked that to the migrant crisis. I could have a debate with J. D. advance about that. But everything else he said in that social media post that the death the manner of the death of this poor young man Henry V Novak uh was symptomatic of the decline of our ci civilization. That was on the money absolutely accurate. Now but I could debate whether or not you know you could call the seek community the result of our migrant crisis. I don't in all honesty think that you can. But I do not want to hear Lammy or Starmmer or any of Labour's other apologists telling us we can't link what happened in Belfast, this beheading horror to the migrant crisis. It is a direct result of the migrant crisis. This guy uh arrived here. We're not sure how we got here, but he was clearly an asylum seeker. After 5 years, he was given indefinite leave to remain. This horror is a direct result of the migrant crisis. >> 100%. This would not have happened if we had proper control of our borders and if we took seriously the public's cry to fix our borders and to remove people who shouldn't be here. If the political class did that as people have been asking them to for 5, 10, 20 years, this would not have happened. It is as simple as that. And you know, when I saw that image of the knife man kind of punching the air with a kind of blood curdling delight, I thought to myself, this is an image for our times. Because what we see here is the murderous intent of one individual, but also the murderous indifference of the state. The state has allowed tens of thousands of unvetted men to come into this country. And you know what? Workingclass communities are sick of play paying the blood price for this. They're paying the blood price for bourgeoa virtue because you have the activist class in their leafy suburbs who never suffer the consequences of their activism. They can live far away from the refugees who they welcome in. It's ordinary workingclass communities who are paying the price. It's women and girls who suddenly overnight have 800 men from god knows where at the end of the street. It's the women and girls who have been raped, and there are large numbers of them, including by a suspected uh rape gang, alleged rape gang overseen by Afghan nationals. And it's men like this man in in in North Belfast who was attacked in the most savage way. Ordinary people are pray paying the price for the failure of the state and for the supposed virtue of the activist set. That's what's going on right now and it's sickening. >> What should we do? And but as you say, it's now inflicted by the same kind of horrors that we've seen in England, in Wales, in Scotland, where we have these unvetted men coming into the country uh given pretty much free reign. And they are visiting atrocity after atrocity on ordinary Brits. I don't think there's anything that the old Tory regime will do. Even though Kemma Badno I think is a pretty good leader but I think it will be a wakeup call for ordinary people. I think around the country people who are already very angry about this situation will be made more angry and they will be determined to get new people into power to fix this problem. I think that's what's going to happen. >> Uh this is breaking news Brendan literally just come on the wires. It's their story of this Sudin asylum seeker, this migrant. Uh the suspect in the Belfast knife attack is believed to have traveled from Sudan to Paris and then from Paris to Dublin on unknown dates before taking a bus to Belfast in February 2023 where he immediately claimed asylum uh before being given uh permanent leave to remain in the UK in September 2023. that is from uh the PSNI Chief Constable John Butcher. That's the police service in Northern Ireland. So, he uh essentially broke into Belfast in February 2023. Uh just a few months later, September, he's granted permanent leave to remain. How many questions were asked about this guy? Obviously, probably none. Now we know the kind of guy that we let casually into this country to live for the rest of his life if he wanted to. Unbelievable, isn't it? >> You know what are we doing? What what has become of Europe? The fact that people can just travel around like this. People who we don't know who they are. We don't know what their intentions are. We don't really know where they came from. Sometimes we don't know their names or their ages. It is psychotic. If I was to travel to New York or Paris or Rome, people would at the border, they would say, "Who are you? Show us your passport. What do you want to do here?" You know, this is perfectly normal checks. And they don't, they are not being carried out on these swarms of people that are moving around Europe at the moment. It is really, really worrying and incredibly dangerous. We now know that for sure. I just think that the withering of our borders is the biggest crisis of our time because it is the thing that is allowing so many men to come to this country and many of whom do wicked things, some of whom don't, of course, and people are incredibly angry about it. You know, the other thing they're angry about, Kevin, is constantly being called racist by the establishment just because they have questions about this regime, just because they want a handle on our borders and better border controls. We are constantly called racists for that. We are called fascists. The bourgeoa activist class is always looking down its nose at us. And they will do the same tomorrow. When people get angry about this, they will do the exact same thing and they will call us racists. So we have no control over our borders. We are gagged from speaking the truth. Our emotions are policed by people like Kier Stalmer who tells us not to feel rage. We are just have our hands tied constantly. And that's the thing that's really infuriating people. >> I'll tell you another thing infuriating me. I was away last week but I followed the Novak saga uh with great interest obviously. And do you know what enrages me is that people say to Stalmer? They say to the police, "Look, two-tier policing is palpable in this country. It is obvious. It happens all the time. Henry Novak died because of anti-white racism in a DEI brainwashed police force." And we say this to them and they look us in the face and they say, "No, you're wrong. There's no such thing as two-tier policing." the uh boss of the chief constip police, guy called uh Alexis Boon, said, "I don't recognize the term two-tier policing." So, that is their way of dealing with this two-tier crisis, just staring us in the face and pretending it doesn't exist. That is incendury. It is a recipe for disaster. >> It's classic gaslighting. That's what they're doing because we can all see with our own eyes what's going on. We've all seen the policy documents that the police have adopted which expressly say that you shouldn't treat people equally, that you should treat people differently depending on their race because non-white people might be suffering certain ways and therefore you need more sympathy. We've seen the policy documents. It's there in black and white. They might call it equity or whatever nonsense phrase they use. We call it two-tier policing and we are dead right to call it two-tier policing. They're gaslighting us. And you know, if it kicks off in Belfast tonight, and I I for so many reasons, I hope it doesn't. I really hope it doesn't because we need such a clear moral statement about what's going on and the government will exploit any instability to to demean everyone as racist and violent. So, I'm really hoping it doesn't kick off, but if it does, it will be because of the pressure cooker effect. We are that the government and the media, in fact, lots of the media are constantly putting a lid on ordinary people's concerns. They're constantly pushing them down and in fact demonizing them, saying, "Stop being racist. Stop being a bigot. You know, what's wrong with people coming here and seeking a better life." They're constantly demonizing what we think, trying to silence us, trying to cancel us. And that builds up this tension and this pressure. And sometimes that pressure explodes in ways that are unpredictable and undesirable. So if there is tension tonight, and I hope there isn't, it will be a consequence of that culture of censorship. How else are people supposed to express themselves? What we need to do after this atrocious event in Belfast is throw open public discussion to a bit more honesty, a bit more truth. Tell the truth about what's happened to our borders. Tell the truth about who's in the country and get a handle on these problems before it gets even worse. >> Brendan, great to have you on the show on a very sad day. Thank you so much. Uh the brilliant Brendan O'Neal there, chief political writer at Spiked Online. Let me know what you think about what he was saying.

videos.transcript: Welcome to Belfast. The capital of Northern Island. A city rich in history, culture and vibrant energy. Discover its stunning landmarks and lively atmosphere. Hi I am Martijn your guide in Belfast. After watching this travel guide you know a lot more about this city to get the most out of your trip. From a museum about the most famous ship in the world... ...to an historic jail... ...an amazing viewing point and everything in between. If this Belfast travel guide has given you new travel tips so maybe you just like it please give a thumbs up or leave a comment under the video. That's the way you can help so other people can discover the video as well and I get to create new travel guides for you in the future. The city is served by two airports and Belfast International is the biggest. It handles most international flights. To travel to the city you can use the airport express 300 buses. The journey takes around 30 to 40 minutes and a single ticket will set you back around €18. At peak times from Monday till Friday there is a departure every 15 minutes. Taxis are more expensive between 40 and 50 for a journey of 30 minutes depending on the traffic and your final destination. And there is the City Airport which is located 5 km outside the city center. It mostly handles domestic flights within the UK and some European routs. Let's start the Belfast travel guide with one of the most visited attractions in town and that's the Titanic Belfast. Of course you know the story about Jack and Rose on the ship the Titanic but it all started here in Belfast in 1907, where the ship was built. In 1912 the Titanic left the harbor of Southampton in the UK to New York where it never arrived. The museum showcases the story behind the world's most famous ship... ...located on the site of the former Harland & Wolff Shipyard where the Titanic was built. The museum opened in 2012 exactly 100 years after the famous ship sank. It showcases for example interactive displays about the Titanic construction, journey and sinking. Besides the museum you can explore the SS Nomadic. This was a tender ship which was constructed in 1911 shuttling passengers and cargo between the shore and for example the Titanic. It is restored and preserved and it is the only surviving White Star Line vessel. Nowadays it is a museum ship that offers you a unique opportunity to explore the maritime heritage. In a moment more about this Museum that gives you an Insight in the Irish history but first the Botanical Gardens. A perfect place to run, chill or walk and during the summer periods they use it as a venue for concerts events and festivals. Over here you will also find the Palm House one of the oldest glass houses in the world dating back to 1840. And inside the park you will also find the Ulster Museum it's Northern Island's largest and most diverse Museum. Showcases a wide range of art, dinosaur skeletons and history of the country. And good to know there's no entrance fee. It is time to go a little bit higher 370 meters to be exactly... ...to The Cave Hill. it's a prominent hill overlooking the city and the surrounding areas. In the Cave Hill Park you will also find the Belfast Castle. It was built in Scottish style and finished in 1870. Nowadays they use it for for example weddings and there is a restaurant inside. My advice is to grab a taxi or maybe you use public transportation to the Belfast Castle. From there it's a 30 to 40 minutes hike to the top of Cave Hill. In a moment I show you this old jail and this wall with a lot of history but first the City Hall... What functions as the city council Civic Building. Inside you can visit an exhibition about the history of Belfast. And in the garden outside you will find memorials and statues and it's also a kind of Park on a sunny day. There are free guided tours but you cannot get your tickets online they are only available in the City Hall on the day of the tour. This is the Big Fish a sculpture made of ceramic tiles telling a story about Belfast in a fun and artistic way. Just walk around it and check all the beautiful designs. In a moment you know more about this big wall with a lot of history but first an impressive building the Crumlin road gaol. It served as a prison between 1845 and 1996. It houses notable political prisoners during northern islands troubles and in a moment more about that. Nowadays there are tours so you can see how the prisoners were held. During the 20th century the troubles disturb most of Northern Island and his citizens. The Catholic and Protestant communities were divided by a big wall which was 6 meters high... ...and authorities close the wall by night to keep them separate. The Protestants wants to stay with the UK while the Catholics just want to join Ireland again. It wasn't until 1998 that an agreement was reached bringing an end to the violence. You can visit the Peace Walls by yourself with a lot of beautiful murals... Or you can book a guided black cap tour . Where a taxi driver will tell you more about the history and show you the impressive places. And don't forget to write your name on the wall . One of the oldest attractions in town is the St George Market. It was built between 1890 and 1896 it's only open three days a week from Friday till Sunday. It features a variety of stalls. Selling fresh products, local crafts and unique gifts. The Cathedral quarter over here is the historic trading center of the city. With cobblestone streets and warehouses... ...and the warehouses are now home to clubs restaurants and bars. It is really the place to be in the evening and night and also on a sunny day like today. In this quarter you will also find the Metropolitan art center with some three exhibitions. And the St Anne's Cathedral also known as The Belfast Cathedral. It has been the place for worship for Christians in Belfast for over 100 years. The foundation stone of the Cathedral was laid in 1899... ...and the building itself continue to grow over the years. It's time to go back in time to 1776. In that year the McConnell's Distillery was founded in Belfast producing Irish whiskey. Despite closing in the early 20th century the distillery's Legacy lives on. Now the legend is restored in Belfast. You can visit The Distillery and learn more about the distilling process and of course you can taste some of the Irish whiskies. An historic Landmark is the Queen's Arcade. The last Victorian arcade in the city. It was built in 1880. In 2019 after many years of planning it was reconstructed to his great grandeur of the 19th century. Inside you will find mainly luxury shops. And something else I've been to many places all over the world but when you like murals and street art Belfast is really your heaven. What's the best time to visit Belfast? I think the summer period : June, July and August. When the weather is relatively mild and the days are longer. videos.title: 10 BEST Things To Do In Belfast | Belfast Travel Guide
videos.description:
Belfast, the vibrant capital of Northern Ireland, is a city brimming with history, culture, and a plethora of activities to suit every traveler.
🏨 Best Hotels In Belfast ➡ https://booking.tp.st/y8rKQzYe
🏠 Best AirBnBs in Belfast ➡ https://vrbo.tp.st/IsfNXKml
🎟 DISCOUNTED Tours & Tickets for Belfast ➡ https://getyourguide.tp.st/TvIqels2

Belfast, the vibrant capital of Northern Ireland, is a city brimming with history, culture, and a plethora of activities to suit every traveler. Whether you're visiting for a weekend or an extended stay, there are countless things to do in Belfast that will leave you captivated.

One of the top things to do in Belfast is to explore the Titanic Belfast, an iconic museum located in the Titanic Quarter. This world-class attraction provides an immersive experience into the history of the RMS Titanic, from its construction to its ill-fated maiden voyage. Another must-visit is the Crumlin Road Gaol, a historic prison that offers guided tours, providing insight into its 150-year history.

For those interested in the arts and sciences, the Ulster Museum is a treasure trove of exhibits ranging from art and history to natural sciences. It is located near the beautiful Botanic Gardens, which is perfect for a leisurely stroll. St. George's Market, one of the best markets in the UK and Ireland, is another highlight, offering a variety of local produce, crafts, and live music.

Belfast's nightlife is equally exciting, with numerous things to do in Belfast at night. The Cathedral Quarter is the heart of the city's nightlife, featuring an array of pubs, bars, and live music venues. The Duke of York and the Crown Liquor Saloon are popular spots for experiencing the local pub culture. For a unique nighttime adventure, consider joining a ghost walk or visiting the Dome in Victoria Square for panoramic views of the city.

For those seeking fun things to do in Belfast without alcohol, the city offers a variety of activities. You can try your hand at axe throwing, enjoy a game of mini-golf, or explore the many escape rooms scattered throughout the city. The We Are Vertigo adventure park and the Andersonstown Indoor Aqua Park are perfect for families or those looking for some indoor excitement.

A Belfast travel guide wouldn't be complete without mentioning the city's rich history and cultural heritage. The Black Cab Tours offer a deep dive into Belfast's past, taking visitors through the city's famous murals and the Peace Walls. For outdoor enthusiasts, Cave Hill Country Park provides stunning views of the city and is said to have inspired Jonathan Swift's *Gulliver's Travels*.

In summary, Belfast is a city that offers a diverse range of attractions and activities. From exploring historic sites and museums to enjoying vibrant nightlife and unique experiences, there are endless things to see and do in Belfast. Whether you're interested in history, culture, or simply having fun, Belfast promises an unforgettable visit.
